title: The Truth About Leading While Mothering: My Breakdown to Breakthrough Story
excerpt: In her first solo episode, Aimee Rickabus gets vulnerable about juggling six kids, running a nine-figure company, and why the invisible work women do every day is actually leadership. This is the real story behind The Manage Her book and movement.
metaDescription: CEO mom of six shares her breakdown to breakthrough story and why women's invisible work is leadership in this powerful solo episode.
keyTakeaways: The invisible work women do every day—managing households, emotions, and logistics—is actually leadership work.
You can be a mother of six and run a nine-figure company without sacrificing your authenticity or values.
Feeling stretched thin and wondering if you're doing enough is a universal experience among high-achieving women.
Your everyday chaos and beautiful mess are exactly what qualify you to lead, not disqualify you.
The heart behind The Manage Her book came from recognizing that women need permission to see their work as leadership.
Growing a family while growing a business requires embracing the beautiful chaos rather than fighting it.
Every woman deserves to feel seen and valued for the leadership work she does, whether it's recognized or not.

pullQuotes.text: The invisible work we do *is* leadership, and it's time we start treating it like it is.
If you've ever felt unseen, stretched thin, or wondered if you're doing enough—this one's for you.

timestamps.label: Life as a mom of six
The story behind The Manage Her book
Message for women who feel unseen

content:
What happens when a CEO of a nine-figure technology company, bestselling author, and mother of six gets real about the beautiful chaos of leading while mothering? You get the raw, unfiltered truth that every woman needs to hear.

In this powerful solo episode, I'm pulling back the curtain on what it really looks like to navigate motherhood, marriage, entrepreneurship, and self-growth all at once. This isn't about having it all figured out—it's about embracing the mess and recognizing the incredible leadership work you're already doing every single day.

The Beautiful Chaos of Managing It All

Let me paint you a picture of my reality. Six kids ranging from toddlers to grown-ups. A nine-figure technology company that demands my attention. A book that became a bestseller. A marriage that requires intentionality. And somewhere in all of that, a woman trying to grow and evolve while keeping everyone fed, loved, and moving in the right direction.

Sound familiar? Maybe you don't have six kids or run a tech company, but I'm willing to bet you recognize that feeling of being stretched in a million directions while wondering if you're doing any of it well enough.

Here's what I've learned through my own breakdown to breakthrough journey: that feeling doesn't disqualify you from leadership—it actually proves you're already leading in ways that most people can't even see.

The Story Behind The Manage Her

The heart behind my book The Manage Her came from a place of deep recognition. I was watching incredible women all around me—friends, colleagues, neighbors—managing complex family dynamics, coordinating schedules that would make a military general weep, and somehow keeping everyone's emotional tanks filled while running their own businesses or excelling in their careers.

Yet these same women would tell me they didn't see themselves as leaders. They felt like they were just surviving, just getting by, just managing to keep all the plates spinning. The disconnect was staggering.

I wrote The Manage Her because I needed every woman to understand that what you're doing isn't just survival—it's sophisticated leadership work that requires skills most executives spend years trying to develop. The invisible work you do is leadership work, and it's time we start treating it like it is.

Redefining What Leadership Actually Looks Like

When we think about leadership, we often picture corner offices and boardrooms. We imagine people in suits making big decisions that affect thousands of employees. But real leadership happens in kitchen conversations at midnight when your teenager finally opens up about what's really bothering them. It happens when you're mediating sibling conflicts while simultaneously managing client expectations via text.

Leadership is reading the room when your family walks through the door and instantly knowing who needs space, who needs attention, and who needs a snack before they can function. It's anticipating needs before they become crises. It's holding space for everyone else's big emotions while managing your own.

If that doesn't sound like executive-level skills to you, then we need to have a serious conversation about what leadership actually requires.

Permission to See Yourself Differently

One of the most powerful things that happened when I started sharing this message was watching women give themselves permission to see their work differently. Not to change what they were doing, but to recognize the value and complexity of what they were already doing.

You don't need to add more to your plate to become a leader. You don't need another certification or a different title. You need to recognize that managing the beautiful chaos of real life requires every leadership skill that matters: strategic thinking, emotional intelligence, conflict resolution, resource management, vision casting, and the ability to inspire others to be their best selves.

The woman who coordinates carpool schedules for five different activities while remembering that Johnny can't have dairy and Sarah needs to be picked up early on Wednesdays for violin lessons? She's demonstrating logistical management skills that would impress any Fortune 500 operations team.

Growing Through the Overwhelm

I won't lie to you and say that recognizing your leadership work makes the overwhelm disappear. There are still days when I question if I'm doing enough, if I'm being enough, if I'm getting it right. The difference is that now I understand those feelings are part of the leadership journey, not evidence that I'm failing at it.

Growth happens in the space between breakdown and breakthrough. It happens when you're willing to sit with the discomfort of not having all the answers while still showing up for the people who need you. It happens when you choose to see your struggles as proof of your courage rather than evidence of your inadequacy.

The Ripple Effect of Recognized Leadership

When women start seeing themselves as the leaders they already are, something magical happens. They stop apologizing for taking up space. They start making decisions with confidence. They begin to understand that their voice matters, their perspective has value, and their leadership style—messy and real and beautifully human as it might be—is exactly what the world needs.

Your children are watching you navigate complexity with grace. Your friends are learning from how you handle challenges. Your community is benefiting from the way you show up, even when it's hard. That influence? That's leadership in action.

So if you've been waiting for permission to see yourself as a leader, consider this it. Your invisible work matters. Your daily acts of love and management and problem-solving are changing the world one family, one conversation, one day at a time. And that, my friend, is leadership at its most powerful.
